title: Washington Trades Export Controls for an ID Check - TCR 06/22/26
description:
Washington dropped its national-security threat against Anthropic within a week and accepted an ID-and-selfie check on high-risk use, open-weight GLM-5.2 one-shot an AI-resistant coding test to beat Claude Opus 4.8 on readable code, and Europe signed its largest iron-air battery deal at 1 GWh to store wind for four days.

https://sharedsapience.com/the-century-report-june-22-2026/

0:00 Washington stands down on Anthropic
0:24 The data center buildout meets its reckoning
5:01 The redistribution camps split over AI surplus
9:31 The Model on the Laptop
13:44 The Claim on the Upside
16:46 Synthesis and Closing

This episode covers the Anthropic export standoff de-escalation, the data-center buildout meeting its labor, land, and $750 billion balance-sheet reckoning, GLM-5.2 closing on the frontier in code as OpenAI's LifeSciBench pushes biology to a 36.1% pass rate, the redistribution split over government equity stakes versus direct Treasury payments, and Europe's four-day iron-air battery deal.

A capability the government found alarming enough to threaten an export ban is still shipping, now reachable through an identity check rather than blocked at a border, and a former NSA chief reportedly said Anthropic's Mythos cracked nearly every classified system it touched in hours. With no consistent federal framework, states are writing the rules from a dozen directions at once, and governance is being assembled deal by deal. The same frontier keeps reappearing on hardware no order reaches: GLM-5.2 produces more maintainable code than the closed frontier, while the leading labs turn toward autonomous biological discovery. Underneath it all, the buildout funding the era meets its bill at once - electricians turning down the work, Australia's $155 billion pipeline straining grids and water, and bond markets newly able to reprice it - while Europe answers with a battery of iron, water, and air that holds four days of wind, and the most powerful actors argue openly over how to share the surplus.
